card.title: Price Anchoring: Why $9.99 Works
card.teaser: Your brain doesn't judge price by value — it judges by the first number it saw. Here's how retailers weaponize that.
card.coverImagePrompt: A gleaming retail price tag showing $9.99 with a faint crossed-out $19.99 beside it, dramatic overhead spotlight on black velvet, cinematic

lessons.title: A number you can't unsee
lessons.body: Walk into a store. The first watch you look at costs $4,500. The second is $300 — and suddenly the $300 watch feels like a bargain, even though you came in wanting to spend $80. That $4,500 tag did its job, and you never had any intention of buying it. Welcome to the quiet science of price anchoring, probably the most weaponized bias in retail.

visual.imagePrompt: A display window with a luxury watch at center, other items fading into soft focus around it, warm neon glow

lessons.title: What anchoring is
lessons.body: Anchoring is the tendency to rely too heavily on the first piece of information you see when making a judgment. Amos Tversky and Daniel Kahneman demonstrated this in a famous 1974 experiment: they spun a wheel showing a random number, then asked participants what percentage of African countries were in the UN. People who saw a high number guessed much higher than people who saw a low one — even though the wheel was obviously random.

keyTerms.definition: A reference number that influences subsequent judgments, even if it's irrelevant
The price shoppers mentally expect for a product based on prior exposure

visual.imagePrompt: A spinning roulette wheel with numbers blurring, a thought bubble above it showing percentage guesses

lessons.title: Why $9.99 beats $10.00
lessons.body: There are two forces at play. First, left-digit bias: your brain reads the leftmost digit first and treats it as roughly the whole number. $9.99 registers as 'nine something,' not 'basically ten.' Second, the crossed-out anchor: if a shirt's tag says 'was $40, now $24.99,' the 24.99 feels cheap — but shown alone, most people would rate 24.99 as a normal full price. Both tricks exploit the same underlying wiring: we compare, we don't compute.

keyTerms.definition: The tendency to weight the leftmost digit of a price more heavily than it deserves

visual.imagePrompt: A giant glowing $9.99 split so the '9' towers over the '.99,' minimalist retail poster aesthetic

lessons.title: The decoy effect seals the deal
lessons.body: Anchoring has a close cousin. A classic Economist subscription experiment by Dan Ariely offered three options: web-only at $59, print-only at $125, and print-plus-web at $125. Almost nobody picked print-only — but its mere presence as a decoy made the $125 combo feel like an amazing deal, and subscriptions surged toward it. Remove the decoy, and most people chose the cheaper web-only option. The irrelevant option did the selling.
lessons.funFact: Prices ending in .99 are so universal that some luxury brands deliberately use round numbers — $2,000, not $1,999 — to signal 'we're above that trick.'

visual.imagePrompt: Three subscription tiers printed on a magazine ad, with the middle option subtly dimmed as a decoy

lessons.title: The first number wins
lessons.body: Price anchoring works because the mind is lazy in useful ways — we don't recalculate fair value from scratch every time we see a price. We scan nearby numbers and compare. Once you notice the trick, you see it everywhere: menus, mortgages, car lots, SaaS pricing pages. You can't turn off the bias, but you can at least ask yourself the sober question behind the chatter: what's this actually worth to me?

quiz.question: What is the anchoring effect?
options: Relying too heavily on the first number you see when making a judgment
quiz.explanation: Anchoring means later judgments drift toward the initial reference number, even when that number is irrelevant.

quiz.question: Why does $9.99 feel meaningfully cheaper than $10.00?
options: Left-digit bias makes us read the price as 'nine-something'
quiz.explanation: The leftmost digit dominates the quick mental read, so $9.99 registers closer to $9 than to $10.

quiz.question: What's the point of a 'decoy' option in pricing?
options: To make another option look like a better deal
quiz.explanation: A decoy is designed not to be chosen — its job is to make the target option look disproportionately attractive.

sources.title: Judgment under Uncertainty: Heuristics and Biases
